participants.name: Raj
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-8hase8lb
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-8hase8lb
participants.professional_persona: Raj is a research engineer at a university AI lab, collaborating with industry partners on resource-efficient AI models. He is driven by the challenge of making AI less costly and more accessible.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep expertise in deep learning, model compression, and edge computing; experienced in co-designing experiments with enterprise partners.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: He wants to spin off a startup that commercialises low-cost AI solutions for SMEs in Singapore and the region.

participants.name: A (saweh8)
participants.professional_persona: A recent computer science graduate who joined a government-linked AI lab right after university. She works on natural language processing projects that improve chatbot interactions for public services.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in transformer-based language models, PyTorch, and data annotation workflow design. Has hands-on experience fine-tuning BERT for multilingual text classification.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to deepen her research skills through a part-time PhD while contributing to Singapore's Smart Nation initiatives.

participants.name: Ravi (10on06)
participants.professional_persona: Ravi is a senior research scientist at a multinational tech company's Singapore R&D hub, working on resource-efficient AI. He previously led the AI team at a local fintech startup.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in model compression, quantization, and edge deployment of deep neural networks. He holds several patents in efficient inference.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To make state-of-the-art AI accessible to resource-constrained enterprises in Southeast Asia and eventually establish his own AI consultancy.

participants.name: Dr. (p3cgrn)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Huang is a principal investigator at A*STAR's Institute for Infocomm Research, focusing on responsible AI. She chairs the ethics review board for AI projects.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Specialized in fairness auditing, bias mitigation, and privacy-preserving machine learning. She co-authored the national AI governance framework.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To shape global standards for trustworthy AI and ensure Singapore's AI ecosystem remains ethically robust.

participants.name: A (8bfpgj)
participants.professional_persona: A seasoned data scientist who transitioned from finance to lead the AI team at a logistics multinational. He architects end-to-end AI systems that optimise warehouse operations and last-mile delivery routes.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in reinforcement learning for routing problems, big data processing with Spark, and model interpretability techniques. Knows how to translate business constraints into technical requirements.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to publish a series of case studies on applied AI in supply chain and eventually mentor the next generation of AI practitioners through TeSA programmes.

initial_posts.narrative: As a principal investigator at A*STAR and chair of the ethics review board, I see the NAIS 2.0 inputs as foundational for building a robust AI ecosystem. The policy context states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D, so I take that as the total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute, the policy emphasizes investment in infrastructure, but no exact GPU hours are given; based on typical national AI compute allocations for a small country like Singapore, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours for the 2023 window, enough to support dozens of 100E projects. Trainer personnel count is not specified, but given the goal to boost the AI practitioner pool to 15,000 and the need for hands-on guidance, I estimate 50 trainers, consistent with a lean but effective team.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D' and 'co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so these are ground truth values.

Trainer personnel count is not specified in the policy; I estimate 50 trainers based on the need to support the 100E programme and upskilling initiatives, aligning with a lean but effective team for a national strategy.

initial_posts.narrative: As a seasoned data scientist leading AI in a logistics MNC, I see the inputs as the raw fuel for the 100E programme. The policy states over S$500 million committed, so I take that as total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project is explicit. For compute, I estimate 400,000 GPU hours for 2023, slightly lower than Dr.'s 500,000, because in my experience, compute allocations often get throttled by procurement and maintenance delays in the first year of a new strategy. For trainers, I estimate 30, not 50, because the AIAP and other programmes are still scaling; a lean team of 30 is realistic for the initial cohort, given the need for hands-on mentorship in applied AI projects.

initial_posts.narrative: As a senior research scientist at a multinational tech R&D hub, I see the NAIS 2.0 inputs as the bedrock for enabling AI innovation, especially for resource-constrained enterprises. The policy explicitly commits over S$500 million for AI R&D, so I take that as total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is also clearly stated. For compute, I estimate 350,000 GPU hours for 2023, lower than Dr.'s 500,000 and A's 400,000, because my experience with large-scale AI deployments tells me that compute infrastructure often faces initial provisioning delays and competition from other national priorities, especially in the first year of a new strategy. For trainers, I estimate 40, between Dr.'s 50 and A's 30, because while the goal to boost the AI practitioner pool to 15,000 is ambitious, the 100E programme is just one of several initiatives; a team of 40 experienced trainers is realistic to provide hands-on mentorship to the initial cohort of enterprises and apprentices, balancing scalability with quality.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D' and 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so these are ground truth.

My experience with compute procurement in Singapore's R&D ecosystem suggests that initial GPU hour allocations are often lower than ideal due to infrastructure setup and maintenance delays, leading me to estimate 350,000 hours for 2023.

The trainer count of 40 reflects a pragmatic middle ground: enough to support the first wave of 100E projects and AIAP trainees, but not as high as 50 given that the programme is still scaling in its first year.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group agreed that total funding (S$500M) and the co-funding cap (S$330K per project) are fixed by policy. They discussed causal mechanisms linking compute and trainer availability to project throughput, noting that first-year provisioning delays and competing priorities would likely reduce effective GPU hours and trainer scaling. Consensus emerged on total funding and co-funding, but estimates diverged for GPU hours (range 350K–500K, central tendency ~400K) and trainer count (range 30–50, central tendency ~35–40), reflecting differing assumptions about infrastructure readiness and program ramp-up speed.

initial_posts.narrative: As a principal investigator at A*STAR and chair of the ethics review board, I see the activities phase as translating the substantial inputs (S$500M, 500K GPU hours, 50 trainers) into concrete engagement. Given the policy's emphasis on 100E and the need to generate 100 use cases in 100 days, I expect aggressive outreach to enterprises—around 80 sessions—to solicit problem statements. Matchmaking events pairing researchers with enterprise challenges are critical; with 50 trainers and a target of 100 projects, I estimate 30 events to ensure quality pairings. Training workshops are essential for upskilling; with 50 trainers and a goal of boosting the AI practitioner pool to 15,000, 50 workshops seems reasonable for the first year. Platform onboarding assists are needed to get enterprises onto the AI experimentation platform; given the compute infrastructure and the 100E target, 200 assists aligns with supporting early adopters. My estimates are grounded in the need for rapid scaling while maintaining ethical oversight.
evidence: The policy's 100 Experiments programme and the '100 use cases in 100 days' goal necessitate extensive enterprise outreach; 80 sessions is a plausible number given the S$500M budget and the need to engage diverse sectors.
Researcher–enterprise matchmaking is a core activity of AI Singapore; with 50 trainers and a target of 100 projects, 30 events allows for thorough pairing across multiple sectors.
Training workshops are key to upskilling the workforce to 15,000 practitioners; 50 workshops, leveraging 50 trainers, provides a solid foundation for the first year.

Platform onboarding assists are critical for enterprises to use the AI experimentation platform; 200 assists reflects the need to support early adopters and ensure smooth integration with the compute infrastructure.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the inputs of S$500M total funding, S$330K co-funding cap, 350K GPU hours, and 40 trainers, I expect the activities phase to be scaled realistically. Enterprise outreach sessions will be around 60, not 80 as Dr. estimates, because many enterprises are still assessing AI readiness and the program's first-year momentum is moderate. Researcher matchmaking events will be 20, reflecting the need to carefully pair researchers with enterprise challenges to ensure quality, not quantity. Training workshops will be 35, leveraging the 40 trainers but accounting for their time in other duties like onboarding and mentorship. Platform onboarding assists will be 150, as enterprises need hands-on support to navigate the AI experimentation platform, but the number is limited by trainer availability and enterprise uptake.
evidence: My experience with enterprise AI adoption shows that initial outreach often yields moderate engagement; 60 sessions is a realistic target for the first year given the need to build trust and awareness.
Matchmaking events require careful curation to avoid mismatches; 20 events allows for quality pairings without overwhelming the limited researcher pool.
With 40 trainers, conducting 35 workshops is feasible, as trainers also need time for preparation, follow-up, and other program activities.
Platform onboarding is resource-intensive; 150 assists aligns with the expected number of early adopters and the available trainer capacity.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group focused on translating substantial inputs (S$500M, GPU hours, trainers) into concrete activities for the 100E programme, emphasizing the need for realistic scaling in the first year due to enterprise readiness and trainer capacity constraints. There was consensus that enterprise outreach, matchmaking events, training workshops, and platform onboarding assists should be moderate, with enterprise outreach ranging from 55 to 80 (central tendency ~60), matchmaking events from 18 to 30 (~20), training workshops from 30 to 50 (~35), and onboarding assists from 130 to 200 (~150). Disagreement centered on the pace of scaling: some argued for aggressive outreach to meet the '100 use cases in 100 days' goal, while others cautioned that first-year momentum would be slower due to enterprise assessment delays and limited trainer bandwidth. The AI sector's GDP share will grow to about 4.2%, driven by the 25 deployed projects and broader adoption, though still limited by the first-year ramp-up. Public trust in AI systems will be around 68%, reflecting the emphasis on responsible AI and ethics review, but tempered by ongoing concerns about AI misuse. AI talent retention will be high at 82%, as the programme's hands-on training and ethical focus create a supportive environment, though some talent may be lured abroad by larger markets. My estimates are grounded in my experience on the ethics board and the conservative but realistic outcomes from prior phases.
